intro:
Judah (Hebrew Yehudah, "praise" or "the LORD be praised") was the fourth son of Jacob by Leah, named at his birth when she declared, "This time I will praise the LORD" (Genesis 29:35). Though not the firstborn, Judah rose to preeminence among the twelve sons, and the tribe descended from him became the dominant tribe of the southern kingdom, the bearer of the Davidic throne, and the line from which the Messiah came. From his name derive the words Judea, Jew, and Judaism — so that the whole covenant people came to be called by the name of this one son.

In the Joseph narrative Judah's character is shown in development. It was he who proposed selling Joseph to the Ishmaelites rather than killing him (Genesis 37:26–27); he is humbled in the episode with Tamar, of whom he confesses, "She is more righteous than I" (Genesis 38:26); and at the climax in Egypt he offers himself as surety and a substitute for Benjamin, pleading to bear the boy's bondage in his place (Genesis 44:33) — a foreshadowing of the self-substituting Lion who would spring from his tribe. On his deathbed Jacob gave Judah the kingly oracle: "The scepter shall not depart from Judah, nor the ruler's staff from between his feet, until tribute comes to him; and to him shall be the obedience of the peoples" (Genesis 49:10).

The tribe of Judah marched first in the wilderness camp (Numbers 2:3) and was allotted the southern hill country of Canaan, including Hebron and, in time, Jerusalem. From Judah came David, anointed king over all Israel (1 Samuel 16:13), and the everlasting dynastic promise of 2 Samuel 7:16. After Solomon's death the kingdom divided, and "Judah" became the name of the southern kingdom — comprising Judah and Benjamin and centered on the temple in Jerusalem — which outlasted the northern kingdom by well over a century before falling to Babylon in 586 BC. The exiles who returned and rebuilt were chiefly of Judah, and the post-exilic province under Persia, Greece, and Rome bore the name Judea.

Theologically, Judah is the tribe of kingship and of fulfilled promise. The prophets keep the Davidic hope alive through the darkest days — a shoot from the stump of Jesse (Isaiah 11:1) and a righteous Branch raised up for Judah (Jeremiah 23:5–6). The New Testament gathers up the whole line in Jesus: Matthew's genealogy traces Him through "Judah and his brothers" (Matthew 1:2–3); Hebrews notes that "our Lord was descended from Judah" (Hebrews 7:14); and Revelation proclaims the triumph of "the Lion of the tribe of Judah, the Root of David" (Revelation 5:5). The praise embedded in his name finds its end in the King whom every tribe will one day confess.
hitchcock_meaning: the praise of the Lord; confession
